content.text: Conventional Commits is a lightweight specification that structures git commit messages into machine-readable tokens. In agentic AI repositories—where prompt templates, evaluation datasets, model versions, and orchestration code evolve in lockstep—this structure becomes the connective tissue between code changes and model behavior changes. A typed, scoped commit that includes prompt and eval identifiers lets teams trace regressions back to the exact change that caused them, automate meaningful changelogs, and maintain an audit trail that satisfies both engineering and AI-safety requirements.
Conventional Commits is a commit message convention that enforces a predictable prefix structure:

. For agentic AI repositories the convention is extended with optional AI-specific footers that link commits to the prompt versions and evaluation suites they affect. This means a single git log contains not just code history but a full timeline of model behavior, prompt evolution, and evaluation drift.
The specification defines a small vocabulary of commit types (
) and extends it for agentic work with additional types:
for evaluation suite additions or modifications, and
for backbone model version upgrades. Footer tokens such as
surface AI-layer metadata directly in the commit.

Agentic AI systems are dual-layer software: there is deterministic code (routing logic, tool implementations, orchestration graphs) and probabilistic behavior (prompt-driven completions, model responses). Traditional git workflows track the deterministic layer well but are blind to the probabilistic one. A refactored prompt that ships with
leaves the team unable to correlate a downstream accuracy drop with its cause.
Conventional Commits closes this gap by making behavior-affecting changes first-class citizens in git history. This matters for several interconnected reasons:
Regression attribution: when evaluation scores drop after a deploy, the typed commit log identifies whether the culprit is a prompt change, model upgrade, or tool modification.
Automated changelog generation: tools like semantic-release and release-please parse commit types to produce human-readable release notes without manual effort.
Cross-team alignment: product, safety, and ML teams share a single source of truth about what changed in each release.
Compliance and audit: regulated industries and safety-critical deployments need traceability from deployed behavior back to reviewed code changes.
Traceable model behavior: every prompt or model change appears as an explicit typed commit with an identifier, not buried in a generic 'update' commit.
Automated release notes: semantic versioning and changelog generation become reliable because the commit type drives the version bump decision.
Faster incident response: engineers grep commit history for prompt-id or eval-ref tags to isolate the change that introduced a behavioral regression.
Reduced review friction: reviewers see at a glance whether a PR touches prompts (requiring ML review), evals (requiring QA sign-off), or only code (standard review).
Integration with tooling: commitlint, standard-version, and semantic-release all natively parse Conventional Commits, enabling full CI automation.
Documentation by default: a well-typed commit history serves as living documentation of every deliberate behavior change ever made to the system.
Initial friction: developers must internalize the type vocabulary and remember to include AI-specific footers; this slows early adoption.
Tooling maintenance: custom commitlint rules for AI-specific types require upkeep as the vocabulary evolves.
Squash-commit tension: teams that squash PRs into a single commit may lose granular type information unless the squash message is carefully authored.
False confidence risk: a commit message is only as accurate as the author; a mislabeled prompt change provides false traceability.
Scope discipline required: teams must agree on what constitutes a valid scope to avoid an explosion of arbitrary scope names.

Conventional Commits works through three interlocking mechanisms: a format convention authors follow when writing commit messages, a lint tool that enforces the convention at commit time via a Git hook, and a changelog generator that reads the typed history to produce release artifacts.
For agentic AI repositories, the flow is extended with an AI-layer hook. This hook inspects commits whose type is
and requires that they carry a

footer. The footer values are validated against the prompt registry (a directory of versioned YAML files) and the model catalogue (a JSON manifest). If the referenced IDs do not exist in the registry, the commit is rejected with an actionable error message.

At CI time, a changelog job reads the entire commit log since the last tag, groups commits by type and scope, and emits a structured CHANGELOG.md entry and a GitHub Release with appropriate semver bump:

attrs.alt: Conventional Commits enforcement pipeline for agentic AI repositories showing commit types, enforcement flow, and example log

content.text: The architecture consists of four layers operating in sequence:
commitlint runs on every commit, checking the type and scope against the project configuration and requiring AI-specific footers on prompt and model commits.
a second hook validates that all prompt-id and eval-ref values in the pushed commits resolve to actual entries in the prompt registry and evaluation catalogue.
on merge to main, the changelog job runs semantic-release or release-please, reads the typed commit log, and produces versioned release artifacts.
the generated release notes include direct links from prompt-id tokens to the prompt registry entry, enabling one-click navigation from a changelog entry to the exact prompt template that changed.
Any repository that manages prompt templates alongside application code.
Teams running automated evaluation pipelines that must be linked to specific code or prompt versions.
Organisations that ship agentic AI features to production and need regression traceability.
Projects where multiple teams (ML, product, platform) contribute to the same repository and need a shared vocabulary for change communication.
Regulated environments requiring an auditable change log that distinguishes behavior changes from infrastructure changes.
Implementation involves four steps: installing commitlint, authoring the configuration, writing the commit-msg hook, and wiring the changelog pipeline.

# 1. Install commitlint and the conventional config
npm install --save-dev @commitlint/cli @commitlint/config-conventional

# 2. Create commitlint.config.js
# (see configuration below)

# 3. Install husky for Git hooks
npm install --save-dev husky
npx husky init

# 4. Add commit-msg hook
echo 'npx --no -- commitlint --edit $1' > .husky/commit-msg
chmod +x .husky/commit-msg

# 5. Install semantic-release for automated changelog
npm install --save-dev semantic-release   @semantic-release/commit-analyzer   @semantic-release/release-notes-generator   @semantic-release/changelog   @semantic-release/git
The following examples show well-formed commits for each AI-specific type:

# Feature commit linking to eval suite
feat(retrieval): add hybrid BM25+dense search strategy

Combines keyword and semantic search using reciprocal rank fusion.
Improves recall@10 by 12% on the internal benchmark.

Eval-Ref: eval/retrieval-v3.yaml
Closes: #412

---

# Prompt change commit linking to registry
prompt(system): tighten refusal boundaries for financial queries

Updated to reject speculative investment advice more consistently.
Regression tested against eval/safety-finance-v2.yaml.

Prompt-Id: prompts/system/financial-v1.7.yaml
Breaking-Behavior: yes – responses to certain financial queries now refuse

---

# Model upgrade commit
model(backbone): upgrade from claude-3-5-sonnet to claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219

Latency p99 decreased by 18%. Tool-call accuracy improved by 4 points
on eval/tool-calling-v5.yaml. No prompt changes required.

Model-Version: claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219
Eval-Ref: eval/full-suite-v5.yaml

A fintech company building an AI-powered transaction classification agent adopted Conventional Commits after a production incident where a prompt change that tightened classification thresholds caused a 15% drop in recall on ambiguous transactions. The change had been committed as
making it invisible in the release notes.
After the incident, the team retrofitted their repository with:
A commitlint configuration requiring the 'prompt' type for any change touching the prompts/ directory.
A pre-push hook that validated all Prompt-Id footers against the prompt registry.
Semantic-release configured to treat 'prompt' commits as patch-level changes with a dedicated section in release notes.
A Slack webhook triggered by new GitHub Releases that posted the changelog to the #ml-ops channel, alerting ML engineers to prompt changes automatically.
Within two months, mean time to identify the root cause of evaluation regressions dropped from 4 hours to 22 minutes because engineers could run
and immediately see every prompt change in the affected window.
this hides behavior-affecting changes in the noise of infrastructure commits. Always use the 'prompt' type for changes that modify system messages, user instructions, or few-shot examples.
a commit message like 'prompt: update instructions' without a scope is ambiguous. Use a scope to identify the subsystem: prompt(system), prompt(user-facing), prompt(tool-router).
entering a Prompt-Id that does not exist in the registry defeats the traceability purpose. Pre-push hooks should validate IDs against the actual registry.
behavioral breaking changes must carry 'Breaking-Behavior: yes' in the footer so downstream consumers know to re-evaluate integrations.
if a PR contains both a prompt change and a code change, squashing into a single commit loses type granularity. Prefer squashing into a message that retains the highest-impact type.
Keep the short description under 72 characters; put elaboration in the body.
Use imperative mood in the description: 'add retry logic', not 'added retry logic'.
Write one commit per logical change; avoid omnibus commits that mix prompt, code, and eval changes.
Always include an Eval-Ref footer on prompt and model commits so the evaluation that validated the change is permanently linked.
Run commitlint in CI as a status check to catch mis-formatted commits in PRs before merge.
Version your prompt and eval files with semantic identifiers (v1.2.0) that map to git tags for full cross-reference capability.
Document the type vocabulary in a CONTRIBUTING.md so new team members understand the extended types without guessing.

Commitlint adds negligible overhead (under 200ms) to the commit workflow. The pre-push registry validation hook scales linearly with the number of commits being pushed, which is bounded by PR size. For monorepos with thousands of commits, the changelog generator may take 30-90 seconds; this is acceptable as a CI step but should not run synchronously in a developer workflow.
At scale, teams split the commit log by directory prefix: tools like
generate per-package changelogs from the same typed commit history without duplicating configuration. The AI-specific footer tokens travel through all scoped changelog pipelines unchanged.
Lint commit messages at commit time
Conventional Commits improves security by making behavior-changing commits visible. When a prompt is modified to remove a safety guardrail, the
footer flag it for mandatory security review. CODEOWNERS rules can require additional approvals on commits that touch prompt files.

Reliability is improved through enforcement: commitlint running in CI means that mis-formatted commits fail the PR status check before merging, preventing gaps in the changelog. The footers also create a durable link between production behavior and the exact commit that shipped it, enabling precise rollbacks.
Beyond the baseline convention, mature agentic AI teams adopt several advanced patterns that further tighten the connection between commit history and system behavior.

A scoped prompt registry maps each prompt file to a semantic version string and maintains a JSON manifest at prompts/registry.json. The commit hook resolves every Prompt-Id footer against this manifest, ensuring only registered, reviewed prompts are referenced. This prevents orphan references that point to deleted or renamed files.

In a microservices architecture where prompts, tools, and the orchestration layer live in separate repositories, teams aggregate typed commit logs from all repositories into a single release dashboard. A GitHub Actions workflow collects the CHANGELOG.md from each repository at release time, merges them by timestamp, and publishes a unified system changelog. Prompt-Id and Eval-Ref tokens appear as cross-repository links, letting readers navigate from a system changelog entry to the exact repository and commit that changed a prompt.
Use a common commit type vocabulary across all repositories in the system.
Store the aggregated changelog in a dedicated 'release-notes' repository or wiki.
Add repository prefixes to footer tokens to disambiguate same-named prompts across repos: 'Prompt-Id: retrieval-service/prompts/search-v2.yaml'.
Automate the aggregation with GitHub Actions matrix jobs triggered on any service release.
Conventional Commits turns git history into a typed changelog that distinguishes code changes from prompt, model, and eval changes.
Extend the standard type vocabulary with 'prompt', 'eval', 'model', and 'tool' types for agentic AI repositories.
Add AI-specific footer tokens (Prompt-Id, Eval-Ref, Model-Version) to link commits to the exact registry entries they affect.
Enforce the convention at commit time with commitlint and husky, and at CI time as a required status check.
Feed the typed history into semantic-release or release-please to produce automated changelogs without manual effort.
The biggest payoff is regression attribution speed: typed commit history lets you find the behavioral change that caused an evaluation drop in minutes rather than hours.
